highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.139 Released: Agent View & /goal Command
highlights.summary: The latest Claude Code release introduces an agent view (Research Preview) that gives you a single unified list of all your Claude Code sessions — whether running, waiting for input, or completed. A new `/goal` command lets you set a completion condition so Claude keeps working autonomously until the goal is met. Run `claude agents` in your terminal to get started with the agent view.

highlights.title: Introducing Claude Opus 4.7
highlights.summary: Anthropic has announced Claude Opus 4.7, the latest model in the Opus family designed for more complex, demanding tasks. If you use Claude Code with the Opus model tier, upgrading to 4.7 may improve the quality of code generation and reasoning on harder problems. Check the Anthropic blog for details on capabilities and how to enable it in your Claude Code configuration.

highlights.title: Show HN: adamsreview – Multi-Agent PR Reviews for Claude Code
highlights.summary: A developer built adamsreview, a Claude Code plugin that runs deeper pull request reviews using parallel sub-agents, multiple validation passes, and persistent JSON state to track findings. It reportedly catches significantly more real bugs than Claude's built-in review capability and can optionally integrate with Codex CLI and PR bot comments. This is a great example of extending Claude Code's capabilities through community-built tooling.

highlights.title: Using Claude Code: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of HTML
highlights.summary: This Hacker News discussion explores how Claude Code excels at generating plain HTML, often producing surprisingly complete and functional results with minimal prompting. The linked examples showcase practical use cases where simple HTML output from Claude Code outperforms more complex framework-based approaches. Worth reading if you want to understand where Claude Code shines without overengineering your stack.

highlights.title: Show HN: I Built a 500K LOC Production App Alone in 7 Months
highlights.summary: A solo developer shares how they built a 500,000-line production application in just 7 months, with a 5-hour uncut live session as proof of their workflow using AI coding tools including Claude Code. The post sparked a lively Hacker News discussion about the realistic scale of what solo developers can achieve with AI assistance today. It's a compelling case study for developers curious about AI-augmented solo development.

highlights.title: Claude Code Just Got a Dashboard
highlights.summary: This YouTube video covers the new dashboard features added to Claude Code, likely corresponding to the agent view introduced in v2.1.139 that lets you monitor and manage all your sessions in one place. If you prefer a visual walkthrough over reading release notes, this video is a quick way to understand how to use the new interface. Great for developers who are visual learners or new to the agent management features.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: A focused 30-minute YouTube tutorial designed to take developers from zero to productive with Claude Code, covering the most important features and workflows in a concise format. This is an efficient option if you want a structured overview without committing to a multi-hour course. Ideal for developers who already know how to code but are new to integrating Claude Code into their daily workflow.

tips_of_the_day: Use the new `/goal` command to define a clear completion condition before starting a long task — Claude will keep iterating autonomously until it's done, saving you from repeatedly re-prompting for the same objective.

Run `claude agents` to open the agent view and get a bird's-eye view of all your active and completed sessions. If a session is blocked waiting for your input, you'll spot it immediately instead of wondering why work stopped.

When asking Claude Code to review a pull request, break the review into explicit stages in your prompt (e.g., 'first check for security issues, then check for performance, then style') — structured prompts like this mimic what multi-agent review tools do and consistently produce more thorough results.
